pages_preview.text_preview: IRB Instructions In order to begin data collection for any SCORE replication protocol, you will need to undergo two levels of IRB review: (1) your usual local IRB process, and (2) a secondary IRB review performed by a Department of Defense Human Research Protection Office (HRPO). This document will give you all the specific language you must include at the local IRB level in order to meet the base requirements for HRPO. Local IRB Language & Requirements Beginning when you submit your local IRB application, there are specific requirements that might differ from your normal procedure. Review this entire document before beginning your local IRB application so that you can plan for the requirements of the secondary review as early as possible. The HRPO will focus on the elements of your IRB application, consent documentation, and approval letter listed below. We understand that every institution will have different processes; please get in touch with the SCORE Coordinators (scorecoordinator@cos.io) if you have any questions about navigating the requirements to your institution. When you have a complete draft of your IRB application and affiliated documents, you can email all of your materials to us for review. A member of our team will be in touch with any required edits, or confirmation that your protocol is ready for submission to your IRB. (1) IRB application form ● You must report the source of funding for this project. Include the following language wherever appropriate in your form: “This project is sponsored by the Department of Defense through an award to the Center for Open Science.” ● Where you discuss how your study will deal with unexpected issues/adverse events/etc., you must include the following required language: “Reporting of unanticipated problems involving risks to subjects or others, suspensions, terminations, serious adverse events, and serious or continuing non-compliance shall be made in accordance with the Department of Defense (DoD) and [YOUR INSTITUTION]’s regulations.”

● You must make your IRB aware that, to the extent possible given any logistic or ethical constraints, the data that you collect will be shared on OSF or another data repository. Your consent form should also discuss your anonymization and data storage plans. ● Report the larger, pooled sample size for the number of participants to avoid having to go through another round of review. ● Save a copy of the form you submit to your local IRB to request review. For some IRBs, this may be an application document that you fill out and send to the IRB via email or a web portal. For others, it may be an online form that you fill out and submit directly. If that is the case, a PDF of the completed webform is acceptable. ● Be prepared to provide your IRB application and all other documents you submit to your IRB (e.g., study protocol, consent form, study materials, recruitment materials... etc.), as we need to submit all of these documents to HRPO. (2) Informed Consent documents ● You must include the following language regarding this project’s funding source near the beginning of your consent form (i.e., within the first few paragraphs): “[This project/protocol/study] is sponsored by the Department of Defense through an award to the Center for Open Science.” ● You must include the following language to inform participants that HRPO representatives might view their data for ethical oversight: “Authorized representatives from the Department of Defense Human Research Protection Program may review and/or obtain information related to your participation in this research study as part of their responsibility to protect human research subject volunteers.” ● Your consent form should also either discuss data anonymization or ask for explicit consent to share identifiable data. Depending on your protocol and data-sharing intentions, you can include language such as: De-identified (anonymous) versions of your data will be shared publicly on the Open Science Framework (https://osf.io) upon completion of the project.

● If HIPAA authorization is required for the study: In the HIPAA Authorization or HIPAA authorization section of the consent form, representatives of the DoD must be listed as one of the parties to whom private health information may be disclosed. ● If your protocol poses greater than minimal risk: You must address (1) whether medical care will be provided and (2) whether compensation will be provided for care sought independently. Per CFR Title 21: "For research involving more than minimal risk, an explanation as to whether any compensation and an explanation as to whether any medical treatments are available if injury occurs and, if so, what they consist of, or where further information may be obtained." (3) Approval Letter ● If your protocol is considered to be exempt research: Make sure that the category of exemption is clearly indicated in your IRB materials. This can be accomplished in your application if you request review of concurrence with a specific category, and/or in the approval letter. Human Subjects Protection Training HRPO requires that every person on your protocol involved in human subjects research submit documentation of human subjects protection training (per local policy). In most cases this will consist of CITI training certificates (however, if your institution does not use the CITI program, you will need to provide documentation that you have completed the human subjects protection training accepted by your institution). Any personnel not involved in human subject research will not be required to submit documentation of human subjects protection training (e.g., if someone will only provide administrative support, or will only assist with data analysis by handling de-identified data only, and will not assist with data collection).

Labs Without an FWA For Labs that do not have a FWA, their protocols are submitted to BRANY, a national organization that provides IRB services, via the following process: ● To submit your protocol on the BRANY webportal, first make sure that you have activated your account via the email that was sent to you. If you have not received that email yet, please let me know. When you have access, log into the BRANY webportal and follow these steps: ● Under "Actions" select "Start a new study", then begin an application for an SBER protocol. ● Once you are in the protocol draft, at the very top of the page there's a button labeled "Collaborators". Please add Tim Errington (tim@cos.io; our director of research) with "Edit, Manage, and Submit" permissions so that our team can also access the protocol. ● Fill out the first page just for your team; nobody from COS should be included as key personnel. The Organization will be your institution, so select "Not Listed" and just add that in the next item. ● Fill out the second page as appropriate. I expect you will be filing for exempt status under either category 2 or 3, but use your discretion. ● From there, it should be fairly straightforward to fill in the rest of the application. You'll see where to upload the SBER Protocol Template, and you can just reference the relevant sections to fill out much of the application itself. The DoD supplement can be uploaded in the same place. ● Once you complete the "Privacy and confidentiality" section, let us know. We will complete the final payment sections and submit the protocol from there. Required Docs: To gain access to BRANY: ● IRB authorization 2017_Study Specific_FILLABLE FORM (rev.20180925).docx ○ Agreement between BRANY and local IRB to defer oversight of the protocol ● 2019-BRANY-User-Access-Form-complete-sign-return-20190806-V2.1.pdf ○ Anyone who needs to be able to access the BRANY webportal to manage protocols must fill out one of these forms to get an account To submit a protocol for review: ● 55.DOD Supplement FINAL 20130913.docx ● BRANY SBER IRB TEMPLATE PROTOCOL_20180517.docx
